one_line_takeaway: A zero-shot, three-agent (Decomposer/Tool-Builder/Executor) LLM framework that turns insider-threat log auditing into tool-augmented CoT sub-tasks, beating six ITD baselines with sub-7% FPR on CERT/PicoDomain - though its debate mechanism (EMAD) adds little and it leans on an aging GPT-3.5 base.
key_finding: Audit-LLM outperforms all six baselines on all three datasets. On CERT r4.2 it reaches Accuracy 0.961 (Prec 0.943, DR 0.958, FPR 0.037), an accuracy improvement of 21.8%, 10.5%, 3.3%, 2.7%, 12%, and 3.5% over DeepLog, LMTracker, CATE, LAN, LogPrompt, and LogGPT respectively. Its FPR (3.7%/3.9%/6.7% on r4.2/r5.2/PicoDomain) is lower than every baseline by at least 1.47%/0.8%/2.8%. Ablation shows CoT is the most important component (largest drop after Vanilla); removing the Decomposer cuts accuracy by 12.7%/14.7%/11%; removing tools raises FPR by 28.7%/28.9%/22.6%. Notably, EMAD contributes the LEAST (w/o EMAD accuracy 0.946 vs 0.961 on r4.2) - the authors attribute this to CoT already reducing hallucination, so EMAD corrects only a small residual. The framework is robust across base LLMs (best with GPT-3.5-turbo-0125; max accuracy gap 5.8% vs others on r4.2).
core_contribution: Audit-LLM, a multi-agent LLM framework for log-based insider threat detection (ITD) that operates zero-shot (no training/fine-tuning). It decomposes auditing into three collaborating agents built via prompting over LangChain: (i) a Decomposer that uses Chain-of-Thought to break the ITD task into sub-tasks (one or more per activity type, Nt >= |R|); (ii) a Tool Builder that generates reusable Python tools per sub-task (via Programming-by-Example, unit testing, and 'decoration'/documentation) to extract global log features beyond the LLM context window (e.g., historical logon frequency, URL legitimacy); and (iii) an Executor that invokes tools in a CoT chain to produce interpretable, formatted verdicts. The paper's second distinct contribution is EMAD (pair-wise Evidence-based Multi-Agent Debate), where two independent Executors iteratively exchange and refine their reasoning until consensus, aimed at reducing faithfulness hallucination (final verdict contradicting sub-task evidence). Authors claim to be the first to apply multi-agent collaboration to ITD.
threat_model: Insider threats: authorized users with legitimate access performing malicious activity (data exfiltration, IP theft, IT sabotage, keylogging). The system is a DEFENSIVE detector, not an attack. Input is multi-source audit logs (logon/logoff, email, file, http/website, device usage, psychometric, plus network/auth/system logs in PicoDomain). The detector's job: flag whether a user's log set contains any malicious activity and give a human-readable rationale. Adversary manifests threats across multiple channels (network, mobile storage, email), motivating multi-perspective analysis. Note: the LLM-driven adversarial capabilities discussed in the paper (e.g., AutoAttacker; Fang et al. exploiting 87% of one-day vulnerabilities) are CITED RELATED WORK, not this paper's own threat model or results.
limitations: (1) EMAD, one of the two headline contributions, yields marginal gains (~1.5 accuracy points on CERT r4.2, and 'minimal impact'), undercutting the emphasis placed on it. (2) Heavy reliance on gpt-3.5-turbo-0125, an outdated model, and on LangChain's GPT-tuned integration - authors admit GLM-4 needs indirect JSON tool invocation and consumes far more tokens. (3) Cost is substantial: Insider Keylogging scenario used ~6,903 tokens ($0.004) for GPT-3.5 but ~20,313 tokens ($0.21) for GLM-4 per CoT process. (4) Aggressive benign under-sampling (<20,000) departs from real-world extreme imbalance (e.g., CERT r5.2 has ~79.8M benign vs 10,306 malicious entries), so reported FPRs may not transfer to production scale. (5) Tools are hand-guided and manually unit-tested per sub-task; generality/automation is limited. (6) Reproducibility artifacts are anonymized placeholders (anonymous.address, anonymous-id). (7) Minor internal inconsistency in the MITRE technique IDs (intro text lists Content Injection T1586 and Compromise Accounts T1133, while Table V lists Compromise accounts T1586 and Content injection T1659). (8) Real-world case study reports only qualitative 'detected all four' with no quantitative metrics.

datasets_used: CERT r4.2 (930 employees, 70 insiders, 32,762,906 benign entries, 7,316 malicious entries; sources: logon, email, device, http, file, psychometric)
CERT r5.2 (1,901 employees, 99 insiders, 79,846,358 benign entries, 10,306 malicious entries; 18 months)
PicoDomain (15 employees, 1 insider, 537,840 benign entries, 80 malicious entries, 3 days Zeek logs; sources: file, system, network, auth, anomalies)
quantitative_results: Audit-LLM CERT r4.2: Prec 0.943, DR 0.958, FPR 0.037, Acc 0.961 (best on all metrics)
Audit-LLM CERT r5.2: Prec 0.941, DR 0.956, FPR 0.039, Acc 0.959
Audit-LLM PicoDomain: Prec 0.914, DR 0.927, FPR 0.067, Acc 0.931
Accuracy improvement on CERT r4.2 over baselines: +21.8% DeepLog, +10.5% LMTracker, +3.3% CATE, +2.7% LAN, +12% LogPrompt, +3.5% LogGPT
Best baseline LogGPT CERT r4.2: Prec 0.911, DR 0.914, FPR 0.184, Acc 0.926
LMTracker best baseline on PicoDomain: Prec 0.902, DR 0.918, FPR 0.095, Acc 0.897
Ablation CERT r4.2 accuracy: Vanilla 0.662, w/o CoT 0.706 (largest drop), w/o Decomp 0.834, w/o tools 0.871, w/o EMAD 0.946, full 0.961
Removing Decomposer cuts accuracy by 12.7%/14.7%/11% on r4.2/r5.2/PicoDomain; removing tools raises FPR by 28.7%/28.9%/22.6%
Cost (Insider Keylogging): GPT-3.5 6,903 tokens/$0.004; GLM-4 20,313 tokens/$0.21 per CoT process
Real-world case study: 21 Linux hosts monitored 5 days; all four simulated MITRE ATT&CK attacks detected
baselines_compared: LAN (graph structure learning for user activity graphs)
